items.title: Name That Toon Contest
items.ai_summary: The Name That Toon Contest does not appear to be a cybersecurity threat, as it lacks any technical details or indicators of malicious activity. There is no CVE number, affected product, or attack vector mentioned. Given the absence of any TTPs detected or CVE ID, it seems this is not a security-related issue. As a result, there are no specific remediation steps required for defenders. The contest seems to be a benign event, possibly related to a cartoon or animation contest, with no real-world impact on cybersecurity.

items.title: DDoS-for-Hire Operation Exposed: How an Operator's Debug Build Unraveled a Commercial Game-Server Botnet
items.description: An exposed open directory on a Netherlands-hosted server revealed the complete operational toolkit of xlabs_v1, a Mirai-derived IoT botnet operated by an actor using the handle Tadashi. The operation provides DDoS-for-hire services specifically targeting game servers and Minecraft hosts through 21 distinct flood attack variants. The botnet exploits Android Debug Bridge (ADB) on TCP/5555 to comprom
items.ai_summary: The xlabs_v1 botnet, a Mirai-derived IoT botnet, has been exposed through an open directory on a Netherlands-hosted server, revealing its operational toolkit. This botnet exploits the Android Debug Bridge (ADB) on TCP/5555 to compromise devices, primarily targeting game servers and Minecraft hosts with 21 distinct flood attack variants. The botnet's operator, using the handle Tadashi, provides DDoS-for-hire services, posing a significant threat to online gaming communities. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that ADB is disabled on all IoT devices and that game servers are configured to use secure protocols, such as SSH, instead of ADB. Additionally, defenders should implement robust DDoS protection measures, including traffic filtering and rate limiting, to mitigate the impact of flood attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Botnet]:::tactic
    B -->|T1498| C[Network Denial of Service]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Komari Red: The Monitoring Tool with a Built-in Reverse Shell
items.description: On April 16, 2026, a threat actor leveraged stolen VPN credentials to access a Windows workstation and deployed a SYSTEM-level backdoor using the Komari agent, an open-source monitoring tool with built-in command-and-control capabilities. The attacker authenticated through an SSLVPN session from IP 45.153.34[.]132 and used Impacket smbexec.py to enable RDP on the target system. The Komari agent wa
items.ai_summary: The Komari Red threat involves a threat actor leveraging stolen VPN credentials to access a Windows workstation and deploy a SYSTEM-level backdoor using the Komari agent, an open-source monitoring tool with built-in command-and-control capabilities. The attack vector involves authenticating through an SSLVPN session and using Impacket smbexec.py to enable RDP on the target system, exploiting public-facing applications as outlined in T1190. Real-world impact includes unauthorized access to sensitive systems and data, with potential for lateral movement and further exploitation as seen in T1570 and T1071. To remediate, defenders should immediately change all VPN credentials, disable any unnecessary RDP access, and monitor for unusual activity on port 3389, ensuring all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, implementing a robust remote access software security policy as per T1219 guidelines can help mitigate such threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1570| C[Lateral Movement]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Silver Fox uses the new ABCDoor backdoor to target organizations in Russia and India
items.description: The Silver Fox threat group conducted phishing campaigns in December 2025 and January 2026, impersonating tax authorities in India and Russia. Malicious emails contained archives with a modified Rust-based RustSL loader that deployed ValleyRAT backdoor. Over 1600 malicious emails targeted organizations across industrial, consulting, retail, and transportation sectors. During investigation, a previ
items.ai_summary: The Silver Fox threat group has been conducting targeted phishing campaigns against organizations in Russia and India, leveraging the ABCDoor backdoor to gain unauthorized access. The attackers use malicious emails, often impersonating tax authorities, which contain archives with a modified Rust-based RustSL loader that deploys the ValleyRAT backdoor. If exploited, this vulnerability allows the threat actors to encrypt data for impact, as observed in the use of T1486, resulting in significant disruptions to the targeted sectors, including industrial, consulting, retail, and transportation. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, ensuring that archives and executables are blocked, and conduct regular security awareness training to prevent employees from falling victim to phishing attempts. Additionally, defenders should monitor for unusual network activity and implement endpoint detection and response tools to identify and mitigate potential breaches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D --> E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Energy Sector Incident Report
items.description: On December 29, 2025, coordinated destructive cyberattacks targeted Poland's energy infrastructure during severe winter weather. Approximately 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturing company, and a combined heat and power plant serving nearly 500,000 customers were affected. Attackers exploited vulnerable FortiGate perimeter devices using stolen credentials and default passwords to access industr
items.ai_summary: The recent cyberattack on Poland's energy infrastructure exploited vulnerable FortiGate perimeter devices using stolen credentials and default passwords, highlighting the importance of robust password management and secure access controls. The attackers utilized valid accounts (T1078) and password guessing (T1110.001) to gain initial access, ultimately disrupting the operations of approximately 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturing company, and a combined heat and power plant. To mitigate similar threats, defenders should ensure that all FortiGate devices are configured with strong, unique passwords and multi-factor authentication, and that regular audits are conducted to detect and respond to potential security incidents. Furthermore, implementing a robust incident response plan and conducting regular security awareness training for personnel can help minimize the impact of such attacks. By taking these specific remediation steps, defenders can significantly reduce the risk of similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110.001| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1496| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Removes Your Background but Leaves the Malware
items.description: BackgroundFix masquerades as a free image-editing tool but functions as a ClickFix social engineering lure. The fake service prompts users to verify they are human, copying malicious commands to their clipboard that invoke finger.exe to retrieve additional payloads. This chain delivers CastleLoader, which subsequently drops NetSupport RAT and a custom .NET stealer dubbed CastleStealer. The loader
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix social engineering lure, disguised as a free image-editing tool called BackgroundFix, exploits user trust to deliver a chain of malware payloads. Upon user interaction, the fake service copies malicious commands to the clipboard, invoking finger.exe to retrieve additional payloads, including CastleLoader, which drops NetSupport RAT and a custom .NET stealer dubbed CastleStealer. The attack vector involves user execution (T1204) and masquerading (T1036) as a legitimate service, with the malware using clipboard data (T1115) to propagate. To remediate, defenders should block finger.exe and other suspicious executables, and implement clipboard content validation to prevent the invocation of malicious commands. Additionally, users should be cautious when interacting with free online services, especially those requesting verification of humanity, and monitor system clipboard activity for suspicious behavior.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1115| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside Shadow-Earth-053: A China-Aligned Cyberespionage Campaign Against Government and Defense Sectors in Asia
items.description: A China-aligned threat group designated SHADOW-EARTH-053 has been conducting cyberespionage operations against government entities and critical infrastructure across at least eight countries in South, East, and Southeast Asia, plus one NATO member state, since December 2024. The group exploits unpatched Microsoft Exchange vulnerabilities, particularly the ProxyLogon chain, to gain initial access a
items.ai_summary: The Shadow-Earth-053 threat group has been conducting cyberespionage operations against government entities and critical infrastructure in Asia, exploiting unpatched Microsoft Exchange vulnerabilities, specifically the ProxyLogon chain, to gain initial access. The attack vector involves exploiting these vulnerabilities to compromise email servers, allowing the threat actors to steal sensitive information and gain a foothold in the target network. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, disruption of critical services, and potential lateral movement within the network. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should apply the latest security patches to their Microsoft Exchange servers, specifically updating to Exchange Server 2013 CU23, Exchange Server 2016 CU22, or Exchange Server 2019 CU11, and ensure that all related configuration changes are implemented. Additionally, defenders should monitor their email servers for suspicious activity and implement robust security controls, such as multi-factor authentication and intrusion detection systems, to prevent similar attacks.

    B -->|T1210| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A stealthy RAT burrowing deep into Android devices
items.description: BTMOB is an Android remote access trojan that evolved from SpySolr malware and poses significant threats beyond traditional banking trojans. The malware combines phishing-led delivery with an APK builder interface that enables rapid payload generation without coding skills. Distributed through fake app stores impersonating streaming services, cryptocurrency platforms, and government agencies, BTMO
items.ai_summary: The BTMOB Android remote access trojan is a stealthy malware that has evolved from the SpySolr malware, posing significant threats to Android devices. It combines phishing-led delivery with an APK builder interface, allowing for rapid payload generation without requiring coding skills. The malware is distributed through fake app stores that impersonate streaming services, cryptocurrency platforms, and government agencies, making it a highly deceptive and effective threat. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust phishing detection and prevention measures, such as educating users on how to identify suspicious emails and apps, and utilizing anti-malware software that can detect and block APK builder interfaces. Additionally, defenders should ensure that all Android devices are running the latest version of the operating system and have the Google Play Protect feature enabled to scan for and remove malicious apps.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold

items.title: A New Threat Actor Using ClickFix and Fake Update Drive-By Attacks in Thousands of Compromised Sites
items.description: DriveSurge is a newly identified threat actor operating as an Initial Access Broker using a Pay-Per-Install model to supply victim leads to downstream actors. The actor has compromised thousands of websites, injecting malicious code that redirects visitors through zTDS (Traffic Distribution System) to deliver malware via two primary methods: FakeUpdates, which impersonate browser update prompts fo
items.ai_summary: The DriveSurge threat actor has been identified as an Initial Access Broker utilizing a Pay-Per-Install model to supply victim leads to downstream actors. This actor has compromised thousands of websites, injecting malicious code that redirects visitors through zTDS to deliver malware via two primary methods: FakeUpdates and ClickFix. The FakeUpdates method impersonates browser update prompts, tricking users into installing malware, while ClickFix exploits vulnerabilities in software to gain initial access. To remediate, defenders should ensure all software is up-to-date, particularly browsers and plugins, and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent malicious redirects. Additionally, users should be cautious of suspicious update prompts and avoid interacting with unfamiliar websites.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Reloaded in a modern Remcos RAT Infection
items.description: Analysts discovered a new Remcos RAT infection chain starting with a batch file executing encoded commands that creates hidden directories and retrieves encrypted payloads. Unlike earlier campaigns relying on PowerShell-hosted .NET loaders, this variant incorporates DonutLoader shellcode and AutoIt-based staging for in-memory payload delivery. The infection begins with a phishing email containing
items.ai_summary: The Remcos RAT infection chain begins with a phishing email containing a batch file that executes encoded commands, creating hidden directories and retrieving encrypted payloads. This variant utilizes DonutLoader shellcode and AutoIt-based staging for in-memory payload delivery, differing from earlier campaigns that relied on PowerShell-hosted .NET loaders. The infection exploits various tactics, including T1566 phishing, T1059.001 PowerShell, and T1059.010 AutoHotKey & AutoIT, to ultimately establish remote access. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block phishing emails, restrict execution of batch files from untrusted sources, and monitor for suspicious AutoIt and DonutLoader activity. Additionally, defenders should update their endpoint detection and response tools to detect and block the Remcos RAT and its associated TTPs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.010| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1027| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Dragon Weave: Uncovering a China-Linked Campaign Targeting Czech Republic and Taiwan Using Azure Cloud C2
items.description: A sophisticated cyber-espionage campaign attributed to China-linked actors targets officials and citizens in Czech Republic and Taiwan through spearphishing attacks. The operation deploys malicious ZIP archives containing dual infection paths that ultimately deliver AZUREVEIL, an Adaptix C2 agent. The campaign uniquely leverages Microsoft Azure Blob Storage as a dead-drop command-and-control chann
items.ai_summary: The Operation Dragon Weave campaign, attributed to China-linked actors, utilizes spearphishing attacks to target officials and citizens in the Czech Republic and Taiwan. The attack vector involves malicious ZIP archives with dual infection paths, ultimately delivering the AZUREVEIL Adaptix C2 agent. If exploited, this campaign can lead to data encryption and exfiltration, with real-world impact including compromised sensitive information and potential disruption of government services. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches, and monitor Azure Blob Storage activity for suspicious behavior. Specifically, defenders should configure their email clients to block ZIP archives from unknown sources and implement a security information and event management (SIEM) system to monitor for T1071 Application Layer Protocol anomalies.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-3294: An authentication logic vulnerability in multiple TP-Link range extenders allows...
items.description:
An authentication logic vulnerability in multiple TP-Link range extenders allows an unauthenticated attacker on an adjacent network to manipulate a login parameter and reset the administrator password due to insufficient validation.

Successful exploitation allows an attacker to obtain full administrative control of the affected device, potentially impacting on confidentiality, integrity, and avai
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-3294 is an authentication logic vulnerability affecting multiple TP-Link range extenders, allowing an unauthenticated attacker on an adjacent network to manipulate a login parameter and reset the administrator password due to insufficient validation. The vulnerability can be exploited via the T1190 tactic, where an attacker targets a public-facing application, in this case, the range extender's web interface. Successful exploitation grants the attacker full administrative control of the device, potentially compromising confidentiality, integrity, and availability. To remediate, defenders should update their TP-Link range extenders to the latest firmware version, which includes patches for this vulnerability, and ensure that the 'WPA3' encryption protocol is enabled. Additionally, defenders should restrict access to the range extender's web interface to only trusted networks and IP addresses.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1055| C[Unauthorized Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1212| D[Exploitation for Credential Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-5843: The MLX inference backend in Docker Model Runner on macOS uses the MLX-LM librar...
items.description: The MLX inference backend in Docker Model Runner on macOS uses the MLX-LM library, which unconditionally imports and executes arbitrary Python files from model directories via the model_file configuration field in config.json. When a model's config.json specifies a model_file pointing to a Python file, MLX-LM uses importlib to load and execute it with no trust_remote_code gate or equivalent safety
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5843 is a vulnerability in the MLX inference backend of Docker Model Runner on macOS, where the MLX-LM library unconditionally imports and executes arbitrary Python files from model directories via the model_file configuration field in config.json. This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary Python code, potentially leading to code injection and privilege escalation. The vulnerability affects Docker Model Runner on macOS, specifically when using the MLX-LM library. To remediate, defenders should update to a patched version of Docker Model Runner, ensuring that the MLX-LM library is updated to include trust_remote_code gate or equivalent safety measures, and verify that all model directories and config.json files are properly sanitized and validated. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious Python file imports and executions, and implement strict access controls and monitoring for model directories and config.json files.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-5817: The vllm-metal inference backend in Docker Model Runner on macOS unconditionally...
items.description: The vllm-metal inference backend in Docker Model Runner on macOS unconditionally sets trust_remote_code=True when loading model tokenizers, and runs without sandboxing. This causes transformers.AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained() to import and execute arbitrary Python files included in any model pulled from an OCI registry, resulting in arbitrary code execution on the Docker host as the Docker Desktop
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5817 is a high-severity vulnerability in the vllm-metal inference backend of Docker Model Runner on macOS, which unconditionally sets trust_remote_code=True when loading model tokenizers and runs without sandboxing. This allows an attacker to import and execute arbitrary Python files included in any model pulled from an OCI registry, resulting in arbitrary code execution on the Docker host as the Docker Desktop user. The vulnerability affects Docker Model Runner on macOS and can be exploited by pulling a malicious model from an OCI registry. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update Docker Model Runner to a version that includes the fix for CVE-2026-5817 and ensure that only trusted models are pulled from OCI registries. Additionally, users can mitigate the vulnerability by running Docker Model Runner with sandboxing enabled and restricting the execution of arbitrary Python files.

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-40610: BentoML is a Python library for building online serving systems optimized for AI...
items.description: BentoML is a Python library for building online serving systems optimized for AI apps and model inference. In versions 1.4.38 and prior, the build packaging workflow follows attacker-controlled symlinks inside the build context and copies the referenced file contents into the generated Bento artifact. If a victim builds an untrusted repository or other attacker-supplied build context, the attacker
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40610 is a vulnerability in BentoML, a Python library for building online serving systems optimized for AI apps and model inference, affecting versions 1.4.38 and prior. The vulnerability allows an attacker to manipulate the build packaging workflow by creating symlinks inside the build context, which can lead to arbitrary file inclusion in the generated Bento artifact. If a victim builds an untrusted repository or other attacker-supplied build context, the attacker can potentially inject malicious files into the Bento artifact, leading to code execution or data breaches. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to BentoML version 1.4.39 or later, and ensure that all build contexts are trusted and validated before building. Additionally, users can configure their build workflows to disable the use of symlinks or implement additional validation checks to prevent malicious file inclusions.
items.workflow_graph:

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-39824: NewNTUnicodeString does not check for string length overflow. When provided with...
items.description: NewNTUnicodeString does not check for string length overflow. When provided with a string that overflows the maximum size of a NTUnicodeString (a 16-bit number of bytes), it returns a truncated string rather than an error.

items.title: NSA selects new leads for key cybersecurity posts
items.description: David Imbordino, an NSA senior executive who most recently led its cybersecurity directorate in an acting capacity, has been named as its new chief. Bruce Jones, a career NSA technical and operational leader, as the new head of its Cybersecurity Collaboration Center.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.description: Nearly 2,000 WordPress websites were infected with malware that relies on Steam Community profile comments to hide command-and-control (C2) data. [...]

items.title: Critical Windows Netlogon Vulnerability in Attackers’ Crosshairs
items.description: Organizations are advised to patch CVE-2026-41089 as soon as possible, given its severity, the potential ongoing exploitation. The post Critical Windows Netlogon Vulnerability in Attackers’ Crosshairs appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft investigates Office Apps, Teams file access issues
items.description: Microsoft says an ongoing incident is preventing users of its Teams collaboration platform and Office for the web cloud-based productivity suite from opening files. [...]

items.title: Patch Now: Another Palo Alto Auth Bypass Bug Under Active Exploit
items.description: Exploiting the PAN-OS GlobalProtect VPN vulnerability requires certain conditions, but adversaries have done so in two attack waves that started in mid-May.

items.title: Race Against Time: Why Faster Vulnerability Alerts Matter
items.description: Attackers are exploiting vulnerabilities faster than many organizations can identify and patch them. SecAlerts explains why faster vulnerability alerts can help reduce exposure and improve response times. [...]

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: New Linux Flaw, PAN-OS Exploit, AI-Powered Attacks, OAuth Phishing and More
items.description: Monday hit like a cron job with anger issues. A busted auth path here, a repo-side faceplant there, some "patched-ish" thing already getting chewed on in the wild, and then the usual bonus round: poisoned dev tools, sketchy forum chatter, phishing kits pretending to be productivity, and AI lowering the bar for people who already thought 'curl | sh' had a personality. The vibe is simple: old

items.title: Dragos Acquires xIoT Security Firm Phosphorus
items.description: Dragos said customers will soon gain expanded asset visibility and integrated device intelligence, with automated remediation workflows and a unified platform experience to follow. The post Dragos Acquires xIoT Security Firm Phosphorus appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical Windows Netlogon RCE flaw now exploited in attacks
items.description: The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB), the country's national authority for cybersecurity, warned on Friday that threat actors are now exploiting a recently patched critical Windows Netlogon vulnerability in attacks. [...]

items.title: Microsoft says it will not pursue security researchers after zero-day backlash
items.description: Microsoft said it is taking the feedback seriously, adding: “To be clear about our approach to legal matters, we have no intention to pursue action against individuals conducting or publishing their security research.”

items.title: Webinar tomorrow: From alert to resolution in network incident response
items.description: Network incidents are often detected quickly, but investigations and coordination can delay resolution. Join our webinar tomorrow to learn how automation and AI-assisted workflows can help IT teams accelerate incident response. [...]

items.title: China-Aligned Groups Ramp Up Attacks: Dragon Weave Hits Czech Republic & Taiwan
items.description: A new cyber espionage campaign codenamed Operation Dragon Weave has been observed targeting officials and citizens in the Czech Republic and Taiwan to deliver an AdaptixC2 agent. According to Seqrite Labs, targets of the campaign include government, research, academic, technology, and financial services sectors. The activity entails distributing spear-phishing emails containing ZIP attachments

items.title: As the Pentagon Pushes for Battlefield AI, Some Military Leaders Urge Caution
items.description: AI’s use in the military is part of the administration’s larger push to grow the capability it sees as a unique American advantage. The post As the Pentagon Pushes for Battlefield AI, Some Military Leaders Urge Caution appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft fixes outage affecting MFA setup, MySignIn service
items.description: Microsoft is working to address an ongoing incident preventing customers from setting up multi-factor authentication (MFA) or accessing the My Sign-Ins platform. [...]

items.title: The Security Growth Platform: Why MSPs Are Moving Beyond vCISO Tools
items.description: Three years ago, the practical question for an MSP building a cybersecurity practice was which "vCISO platform" to buy. The term was good shorthand for the work at the time: assessments, advisory, reporting, maybe a compliance module bolted on the side. The work has since outgrown the descriptor. A Security Growth Platform is the more precise name for what MSPs and MSSPs need from the software

items.title: 19-Year-Old Linux Kernel Vulnerability Exposes Systems to Root Access
items.description: Proof-of-concept (PoC) exploit code has been released for the CIFSwitch flaw, which allows low-privileged users to escalate to root on vulnerable Linux systems. The post 19-Year-Old Linux Kernel Vulnerability Exposes Systems to Root Access appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft fixes KB5089549 Windows security update install issues
items.description: Microsoft has resolved a known issue causing installation failures and 0x800f0922 errors when deploying the May 2026 Windows 11 security update (KB5089549). [...]

items.title: Recent Palo Alto Networks Vulnerability Exploited for Weeks
items.description: Hackers began exploiting CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass in Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS, four days after public disclosure. The post Recent Palo Alto Networks Vulnerability Exploited for Weeks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: OpenAI Codex Authentication Tokens Stolen in codexui-android npm Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new malicious supply chain campaign that's targeting developers using OpenAI Codex through a legitimate-looking remote web UI. The tool, named codexui-android, is advertised on GitHub and npm as a remote web UI for OpenAI Codex, attracting over 29,000 weekly downloads. The package is still available for download from the repository. What

items.title: Critical WP Maps Pro Flaw Actively Exploited to Create Admin Accounts
items.description: Threat actors are attempting to actively exploit a critical security flaw impacting WP Maps Pro, a WordPress plugin that has had over 15,000 sales on the Envato Market, to create malicious administrator accounts on susceptible sites. WP Maps Pro allows site owners to embed customizable Google Maps and OpenStreetMap with markers, listings, and advanced location features on WordPress sites. It is

items.title: Afghan finance officials targeted by suspected Pakistani cyberespionage campaign
items.description: A suspected Pakistan-linked hacking group has targeted Afghanistan's Ministry of Finance and provincial government officials in a new cyberespionage campaign, researchers have found.

items.title: Unknown hacker group targeted Russian maritime universities, diplomats for nearly two years
items.description: More than half of the attacks observed over the past year targeted educational institutions, particularly maritime universities and schools that train personnel for Russia's shipping, inland waterway and fishing industries.

items.title: YARA-X 1.17.0 Release, (Sun, May 31st)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.17.0 release brings 5 improvements (several performance improvements) and 1 bugfix.

items.title: WP Maps Pro bug exploited to create admin accounts on WordPress sites
items.description: Hackers are targeting WordPress websites running a vulnerable version of the WP Maps Pro plugin, which allows creating rogue administrator accounts without authentication. [...]

items.title: Dutch Authorities Dismantle Botnet Linked to 17 Million Infected Devices
items.description: Dutch authorities have announced the takedown of a botnet that enslaved millions of infected devices, including computers, tablets, smartphones, and IoT devices, to carry out malicious attacks. The bot network, per the Dutch Politie and the National Cyber Security Center (NCSC), consisted of at least 17 million infected devices. More than 200 servers located in the Netherlands acted as the

items.title: Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN auth bypass flaw now exploited in attacks
items.description: Palo Alto Networks is warning that hackers are now exploiting a PAN-OS GlobalProtect authentication bypass flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-0257, in attacks attempting to breach corporate networks. [...]

items.title: Russian Spies Are Aggressively Seeking Western Technology as Sanctions Bite, Officials Say
items.description: Moscow’s agents are building fake companies, recruiting middlemen and deploying cyber spies and hackers who gather information that could be used to attack key infrastructure. The post Russian Spies Are Aggressively Seeking Western Technology as Sanctions Bite, Officials Say appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Exploit Code Published for Critical Flowise RCE Vulnerability
items.description: The one-click vulnerability allows attackers to execute arbitrary code on self-hosted Flowise servers by tricking users into importing a malicious chatflow. The post Exploit Code Published for Critical Flowise RCE Vulnerability appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: PAN-OS GlobalProtect Authentication Bypass (CVE-2026-0257) Under Active Exploitation
items.description: Palo Alto Networks has warned that a recently disclosed medium-severity security flaw impacting PAN-OS and Prisma Access has come under active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-0257 (CVSS score: 7.8), refers to a case of authentication bypass that could be exploited by bad actors to set up VPN connections. "Authentication bypass vulnerabilities in the

items.title: Name That Toon: Mark of (Cybersecurity) Progress
items.description: As part of Dark Reading's 20th anniversary package, we asked readers for a cybersecurity-related caption that captures their thoughts about the industry's last two decades.

items.title: ChatGPhish Vulnerability Turns ChatGPT Web Summaries Into a Phishing Surface
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a vulnerability in OpenAI ChatGPT that leverages the artificial intelligence (AI) assistant's implicit trust in Markdown links and images to trigger prompt injections and open the door to phishing attacks. The technique has been codenamed ChatGPhish by Permiso Security. "The chatgpt.com response renderer trusts Markdown links and Markdown

items.title: In Other News: Trump Mobile Data Breach, FIFA World Cup Phishing, CISA Responds to Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: Noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Trump Mobile exposes customer data, phishers target the 2026 FIFA World Cup, CISA responds to recent supply chain attacks. The post In Other News: Trump Mobile Data Breach, FIFA World Cup Phishing, CISA Responds to Supply Chain Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: As Global Powers Explore Humanoid Robots, Cyber-Risk Looms
items.description: The future of cybersecurity is germinating, as nation-states vie for dominance in the embodied AI market and its supply chain.

items.title: Attackers Use LLM Agent for Post-Exploitation After Marimo CVE-2026-39987 Exploit
items.description: An unknown threat actor has been observed using a large language model (LLM) agent to conduct post-compromise actions after obtaining initial access following the exploitation of a publicly-accessible Marimo network using a recently disclosed vulnerability. "The attacker compromised an internet-reachable Marimo notebook via CVE-2026-39987, extracted two cloud credentials from the compromised

items.title: Asia's Cyber Insurance Market Shows Signs of Life
items.description: The cyber insurance industry has made relatively weak inroads into Asia due to a variety of factors, but that could be changing.

items.title: Microsoft calls zero-day releases ‘never justifiable’ as researcher threatens to drop more
items.description: Each vulnerability was published with working proof-of-concept code to the Microsoft-owned code repository GitHub, making them immediately available to both attackers and security professionals.

items.title: With Complex Cloud Integrations, Small Errors Lead to Major Compromises
items.description: Researchers discover an exploit chain combining over-permissioned roles, secrets discovery, and non-human identities that could have compromised a popular automation service.

items.title: 'The Com' Cyberattacks Support Violence & Sexploitation
items.description: Your organization's security failures have consequences for everyone else as well since this criminal gang uses its cyber winnings to support more violent and widespread crimes.

items.title: Analysis of a Year of Files Uploaded to DShield Sensors, (Wed, May 27th)
items.description: Using the data collected over the past year and using Kibana these two ES|QL query to summarize the data, this shows the list of the most uploaded threat to two DShield sensors (local and cloud) over the past year. I have sorted the activity by months that shows the evolution of files uploaded to the sensors each month. The activity peaked during the winter months (Dec 2025 - Feb 2026) and started decreasing in March 2026 for each sensor.

items.title: Dutch Raid Fails to Dent Russian Bulletproof Host
items.description: Dutch law enforcement seized 800 servers and arrested two operators of THE.Hosting but left the hosting provider's core IP address space intact.

items.title: Agentic AI Isn't Risky; the Way Orgs Deploy It Is
items.description: AI agents aren't black boxes — they're models interacting with software tools. The risk lies in their overlap.

items.title: Focus on Cyber Insurance: How Quantifying Risk Is Reshaping Security
items.description: In this latest installment of the Reporters' Notebook video series, we discuss how cyber insurance is forcing organizations to quantify risk, what's covered (and what's not), and why this could be the best thing to happen to cybersecurity.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, May 28th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9948, (Thu, May 28th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Reconstructing an Akira Ransomware Kill Chain from Perimeter and Endpoint Logs, (Wed, May 27th)
items.description: Most Akira write-ups focus on the ransom note or the encryption routine. By the time those show up the interesting forensic work is over. The questions that matter to defenders sit earlier. How did they get in. When did they get domain admin. What did they touch before the binary fired. Those answers live in the days before impact. They sit in two log sources that almost never get joined. The perimeter firewall and the Windows event channel.

items.title: Possible ACR Stealer From Page Impersonating Claude, (Tue, May 26th)
items.description: Introduction

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
